mission.title: Install and Configure Kcp on Kubernetes (Operator-Based)
mission.description: kcp is a Kubernetes-like control plane designed for managing multiple isolated clusters, known as workspaces. The kcp-operator is the recommended way to deploy kcp in production: it offers more features than plain Helm charts and can actively reconcile missing or changed resources on its own. This mission installs kcp using the operator-based method, including cert-manager for TLS, etcd-druid for etcd management, and the kcp-operator for deploying RootShard, FrontProxy, and admin access.

steps.title: Install cert-manager
steps.description:
Install cert-manager to handle TLS certificate lifecycle management for kcp components:
```bash
helm repo add jetstack https://charts.jetstack.io
helm repo update
helm upgrade --install --namespace cert-manager --create-namespace --version v1.18.2 --set crds.enabled=true --atomic cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager
```
commands: helm upgrade --install --namespace cert-manager --create-namespace --version v1.18.2 --set crds.enabled=true --atomic cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager

steps.title: Install etcd-druid operator
steps.description:
Install the etcd-druid operator from the Gardener OCI registry to manage etcd clusters for kcp:
```bash
helm install etcd-druid oci://europe-docker.pkg.dev/gardener-project/releases/charts/gardener/etcd-druid --namespace etcd-druid --create-namespace --version v0.33.0
```

steps.title: Install kcp-operator
steps.description:
Add the kcp Helm repository and install the kcp-operator:
```bash
helm repo add kcp https://kcp-dev.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
helm upgrade --install --create-namespace --namespace kcp-operator kcp-operator kcp/kcp-operator
```

steps.title: Create certificate issuer
steps.description:
Create a self-signed certificate issuer for kcp TLS certificates:
```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Issuer
metadata:
  name: selfsigned
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  selfSigned: {}
EOF
```
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Issuer
metadata:
  name: selfsigned
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  selfSigned: {}
EOF

steps.title: Deploy RootShard
steps.description:
Deploy the kcp RootShard custom resource with embedded cache. Replace 'kcp.example.com' with your actual hostname:
```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: RootShard
metadata:
  name: root
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  external:
    hostname: kcp.example.com
    port: 6443
  certificates:
    issuerRef:
      group: cert-manager.io
      kind: Issuer
      name: selfsigned
  cache:
    embedded:
      enabled: true
  etcd:
    endpoints:
      - http://etcd.kcp-system.svc.cluster.local:2379
EOF
```
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: RootShard
metadata:
  name: root
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  external:
    hostname: kcp.example.com
    port: 6443
  certificates:
    issuerRef:
      group: cert-manager.io
      kind: Issuer
      name: selfsigned
  cache:
    embedded:
      enabled: true
  etcd:
    endpoints:
      - http://etcd.kcp-system.svc.cluster.local:2379
EOF

steps.title: Deploy FrontProxy
steps.description:
Deploy the kcp FrontProxy custom resource to expose the kcp API:
```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: FrontProxy
metadata:
  name: frontproxy
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  rootShard:
    ref:
      name: root
  serviceTemplate:
    spec:
      type: LoadBalancer
EOF
```
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: FrontProxy
metadata:
  name: frontproxy
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  rootShard:
    ref:
      name: root
  serviceTemplate:
    spec:
      type: LoadBalancer
EOF

steps.title: Generate admin kubeconfig
steps.description:
Create a Kubeconfig custom resource to generate admin credentials, then extract the kubeconfig from the resulting secret:
```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: Kubeconfig
metadata:
  name: kcp-admin
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  username: kcp-admin
  groups:
    - system:kcp:admin
  validity: 8766h
  secretRef:
    name: admin-kubeconfig
  target:
    frontProxyRef:
      name: frontproxy
EOF

kubectl get secret admin-kubeconfig --namespace kcp-system -o jsonpath='{.data.kubeconfig}' | base64 -d > kcp-admin.kubeconfig
export KUBECONFIG=$(pwd)/kcp-admin.kubeconfig
```
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: Kubeconfig
metadata:
  name: kcp-admin
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  username: kcp-admin
  groups:
    - system:kcp:admin
  validity: 8766h
  secretRef:
    name: admin-kubeconfig
  target:
    frontProxyRef:
      name: frontproxy
EOF
kubectl get secret admin-kubeconfig --namespace kcp-system -o jsonpath='{.data.kubeconfig}' | base64 -d > kcp-admin.kubeconfig

steps.title: Verify kcp is accessible
steps.description:
Verify the kcp installation by checking the health endpoint and listing shards:
```bash
curl -k https://kcp.example.com:6443/healthz
KUBECONFIG=kcp-admin.kubeconfig kubectl get shards
KUBECONFIG=kcp-admin.kubeconfig kubectl ws
```

resolution.summary: A successful operator-based installation of kcp will show the kcp-operator, RootShard, and FrontProxy pods running. The admin kubeconfig extracted from the Kubeconfig CR secret enables access to the kcp API. You can verify by running 'kubectl get shards' which should show the root shard, and 'kubectl ws' to list workspaces.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add jetstack https://charts.jetstack.io
helm repo update
helm upgrade --install --namespace cert-manager --create-namespace --version v1.18.2 --set crds.enabled=true --atomic cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager

helm repo add kcp https://kcp-dev.github.io/helm-charts
helm upgrade --install --create-namespace --namespace kcp-operator kcp-operator kcp/kcp-operator

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Issuer
metadata:
  name: selfsigned
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  selfSigned: {}
EOF

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: RootShard
metadata:
  name: root
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  external:
    hostname: kcp.example.com
    port: 6443
  certificates:
    issuerRef:
      group: cert-manager.io
      kind: Issuer
      name: selfsigned
  cache:
    embedded:
      enabled: true
  etcd:
    endpoints:
      - http://etcd.kcp-system.svc.cluster.local:2379
EOF

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: FrontProxy
metadata:
  name: frontproxy
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  rootShard:
    ref:
      name: root
  serviceTemplate:
    spec:
      type: LoadBalancer
EOF

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: operator.kcp.io/v1alpha1
kind: Kubeconfig
metadata:
  name: kcp-admin
  namespace: kcp-system
spec:
  username: kcp-admin
  groups:
    - system:kcp:admin
  validity: 8766h
  secretRef:
    name: admin-kubeconfig
  target:
    frontProxyRef:
      name: frontproxy
EOF

kubectl get secret admin-kubeconfig --namespace kcp-system -o jsonpath='{.data.kubeconfig}' | base64 -d > kcp-admin.kubeconfig
KUBECONFIG=kcp-admin.kubeconfig kubectl get shards

uninstall.title: Remove admin kubeconfig and Kubeconfig CR
uninstall.description:
Delete the Kubeconfig custom resource and the generated admin kubeconfig file.
```bash
kubectl delete kubeconfig kcp-admin --namespace kcp-system
kubectl delete secret admin-kubeconfig --namespace kcp-system
rm -f kcp-admin.kubeconfig
```

uninstall.title: Remove FrontProxy and RootShard
uninstall.description:
Delete the FrontProxy and RootShard custom resources. The kcp-operator will clean up the associated pods and services.
```bash
kubectl delete frontproxy frontproxy --namespace kcp-system
kubectl delete rootshard root --namespace kcp-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove certificate issuer and namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the self-signed issuer and the kcp-system namespace.
```bash
kubectl delete issuer selfsigned --namespace kcp-system
kubectl delete namespace kcp-system
```

uninstall.title: Uninstall kcp-operator
uninstall.description:
Remove the kcp-operator Helm release and its namespace.
```bash
helm uninstall kcp-operator --namespace kcp-operator
kubectl delete namespace kcp-operator
```

uninstall.title: Uninstall etcd-druid
uninstall.description:
Remove the etcd-druid Helm release and its namespace.
```bash
helm uninstall etcd-druid --namespace etcd-druid
kubectl delete namespace etcd-druid
```

uninstall.title: Uninstall cert-manager (optional)
uninstall.description:
Remove cert-manager if it is no longer needed by other workloads.
```bash
helm uninstall cert-manager --namespace cert-manager
kubectl delete namespace cert-manager
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current kcp custom resources and configuration.
```bash
kubectl get rootshard,frontproxy,kubeconfig --namespace kcp-system -o yaml > kcp-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade kcp-operator
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the kcp-operator to the latest version. The operator will reconcile the RootShard and FrontProxy resources automatically.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade --namespace kcp-operator kcp-operator kcp/kcp-operator
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade etcd-druid (if needed)
upgrade.description:
Upgrade etcd-druid to a newer version if required.
```bash
helm upgrade etcd-druid oci://europe-docker.pkg.dev/gardener-project/releases/charts/gardener/etcd-druid --namespace etcd-druid --version <new-version>
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that all kcp pods are running the new version and the shards are healthy.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kcp-operator
kubectl get pods --namespace kcp-system
KUBECONFIG=kcp-admin.kubeconfig kubectl get shards
```

troubleshooting.title: RootShard not becoming ready
troubleshooting.description:
If the RootShard is stuck in a non-ready state, check the kcp-operator logs and the RootShard status conditions.
```bash
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=kcp-operator --namespace kcp-operator
kubectl describe rootshard root --namespace kcp-system
```

troubleshooting.title: FrontProxy LoadBalancer pending
troubleshooting.description:
If the FrontProxy service is stuck with a pending external IP, ensure your cluster supports LoadBalancer services or switch to NodePort.
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kcp-system
kubectl describe frontproxy frontproxy --namespace kcp-system
```

troubleshooting.title: cert-manager issuer not ready
troubleshooting.description:
If the certificate issuer is not becoming ready, check cert-manager logs for errors.
```bash
kubectl get issuer --namespace kcp-system
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=cert-manager --namespace cert-manager
```

troubleshooting.title: etcd connection failures
troubleshooting.description:
If the RootShard reports etcd connection issues, verify the etcd pods are running and the endpoint is correct.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kcp-system -l app=etcd-statefulset
kubectl logs -l app=etcd-statefulset --namespace kcp-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Admin kubeconfig secret not created
troubleshooting.description:
If the admin-kubeconfig secret is not appearing, check the Kubeconfig CR status and ensure the FrontProxy is ready.
```bash
kubectl describe kubeconfig kcp-admin --namespace kcp-system
kubectl get frontproxy frontproxy --namespace kcp-system
```

troubleshooting.title: CRDs not installed
troubleshooting.description:
If the operator reports missing CRDs, ensure the kcp-operator Helm chart was installed correctly. You can also apply CRDs manually.
```bash
kubectl get crd | grep kcp
kubectl get crd | grep druid
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher with Helm 3 and kubectl installed. The cluster must support LoadBalancer services (or use NodePort as an alternative). cert-manager, etcd-druid, and the kcp-operator will be installed as part of the mission steps.
